title: Everywhere and Nowhere: Contemporary Feminism in the United States By Jo Reger Oxford University Press. 2012. 256 pages. $24.95 paperback
abstract: Understanding how the contemporary political period affects social movements is an ongoing task for social-movement scholars. This is particularly true for those who study feminism, as by many accounts feminism, at least in the United States, is at best hopelessly fractured, at worst officially dead. Jo Reger contributes her voice to this topic in her book Everywhere and Nowhere: Contemporary Feminism in the United States. Reger inserts much-needed empirical data into the discussion of contemporary US feminist practice and identity. She found, among other things, that feminists have successfully incorporated differences around sexualities and gender expression into their practice but still struggle with issues around class and race. In this review, I will focus on the bulk of her book—generational and community-level differences within contemporary feminism—suggesting how her work can inform the wider literature around social movements. Reger uses the concept of political generations, which are the “result of experiences that bring together a group of people and politically ‘awaken’ them at the same time” (17), to examine the difference between women who came of age in the 1970s (the “second wave” women's movement) and those who came of age in the 1990s (the “third wave” women's movement). Rather than assuming that these two generations are isolated and/or antagonistic, as many analysts do, Reger instead describes “overlapping generations of feminism” (cf. Whittier 1995; Henry 2004). At any one moment, multiple generations of feminists coexist, learning from and influencing one another, producing important differences and continuities between generations.
